title: Facebook automation erasing previous pull?
excerpt: I am running Facebook Ads integrations for my ad accounts but every time they pull new data for ads that are running, they erase info from ads that have run. Is there any way to save the previous pull automatically/with …

posts.author: RoWest (Ro West)
posts.cookedHtml:
I am running Facebook Ads integrations for my ad accounts but every time they pull new data for ads that are running, they erase info from ads that have run. Is there any way to save the previous pull automatically/with an automation?

I was aiming to use the intergration to keep a running total for my ad budget, but if it is continually deleting sums, it is not actually helpful.

posts.author: DelorSani (DelorSani)
posts.cookedHtml: I’ve noticed the same thing with Facebook automation. It can be frustrating when the system erases previous pulls, especially when you’re trying to keep a consistent flow of content.

posts.author: Sweely (Nabil El Alaoui Sossey)
posts.cookedHtml:
You’re right - if it keeps deleting the historical data, the integration becomes counterproductive for financial tracking.

The issue you and DelorSani are experiencing likely stems from how monday’s direct or standard integration with the Meta Ads API is programmed to handle updates, often designed to synchronize the current state rather than append historical logs.

This type of integration usually updates the same item or row to reflect the latest figures, which is efficient for real-time status but disastrous for cumulative data.

To solve this and ensure you keep a permanent, running total, you need to implement a more sophisticated, server-side data tracking setup, which is where the combination of a custom Monday API connection, the Facebook Conversions API, Google Tag Manager, and a server-side tool like Stape or Google Cloud Platform comes in.

Here is why this combination is a solid solution: instead of relying on the standard, possibly overwriting, monday integration, you can use the Monday API to programmatically create a new item or log an update that specifically records the data and prevents it from being deleted.

The key is to structure your board so that each entry represents a daily or hourly budget/spend snapshot rather than the ad itself, thus preserving historical values.

To get the necessary ad performance data reliably, you would leverage the Facebook Conversions API (CAPI), which is designed for robust, server-side data sharing, sending events like Purchase or Lead directly to Facebook’s servers, which is generally more resilient and accurate than the browser-based Pixel.

You would send your own custom budget or spend events using the CAPI.

Google Tag Manager (GTM) is the control layer; you can use it on your website to define what data to collect and, crucially, instead of sending everything directly to the Facebook Pixel, you configure it to send the data to a server-side tagging environment, which is where a service like Stape or your own server on a platform like Google Cloud Platform (GCP) comes into play.

By using a server-side solution like Stape or GCP, you essentially create a proxy server.

This server receives the data from GTM and then forwards it to both the Facebook CAPI and, more importantly for your need, potentially to another endpoint (like a custom webhook built using the Monday API) that you control.

This gives you granular control over what data is logged into monday and how it is structured - ensuring that instead of overwriting, you are appending a new log entry for your budget every time an update occurs, finally allowing you to maintain that consistent, running total you need.

This advanced method ensures data persistence, provides a robust, non-browser-dependent connection to Facebook, and grants you the necessary automation power via the Monday API to create those vital historical records.
